notable.notable_blurb: Hartford Nook Farm humorist; Huckleberry Finn author

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His Hartford home 1874-1891, at the center of the Nook Farm literary community.
Resident (1874-1891); Mark Twain built the famous Mark Twain House at Nook Farm in 1874 — preserved as a museum today.

cross_connections.display_label: Nook Farm neighbor; Isabella helped establish the community
Nook Farm neighbor; Hooker helped found the community
Hartford Nook Farm neighbor; Stowe arrived a decade earlier
Saved Twain's Hartford house from demolition in 1929
Twain secured Gillette's 1875 Boston Globe Theatre debut
Charles Dudley Warner co-authored The Gilded Age 1873
Her husband's brother co-authored Gilded Age at Nook Farm

narrative_blocks.header: Created the modern American voice in fiction
narrative_blocks.body: Born Samuel Langhorne Clemens in Missouri 1835, he became the era's most celebrated humorist, lecturer, and novelist. Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1885) is widely considered the first major American novel written in vernacular speech.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford Nook Farm home for seventeen years
narrative_blocks.body: He built his Tiffany-decorated mansion at 351 Farmington Avenue in 1874 and lived there until 1891, raising three daughters in years he later called 'the happiest of my life.' His Nook Farm neighbors included John Hooker, Isabella Beecher Hooker, Harriet Beecher Stowe, and Charles Dudley Warner.

narrative_blocks.header: Bankrolled William Gillette's first acting job
narrative_blocks.body: He financed and arranged William Hooker Gillette's stage debut at the Boston Globe Theatre in 1875 when no other patron would back him. Gillette would go on to become America's most celebrated stage actor; Twain remained his patron and friend for life.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-wrote The Gilded Age with his Hartford neighbor
narrative_blocks.body: He and Charles Dudley Warner co-authored The Gilded Age (1873), the satirical novel whose title became the name for the entire post-Civil-War era. Warner was Twain's daily walking companion and editor at the Hartford Courant.

narrative_blocks.header: Stowe's mental decline distressed his Hartford years
narrative_blocks.body: His neighbor Harriet Beecher Stowe lived next door at Forest and Farmington Avenues during her decades of dementia in old age. He visited her often and was painfully affected by her gradual loss of self in the years before his own move from Hartford in 1891.
